**Saskatchewan**[[a]](#cite_note-10) is a [province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") in [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada"). It is bordered to the west by [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), to the north by the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"), to the east by [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), to the northeast by [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut"), and to the south by the United States ([Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") and [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota")). Saskatchewan and neighbouring Alberta are the only [landlocked](/wiki/Landlocked "Landlocked") provinces in Canada. In 2025, Saskatchewan's population was estimated at 1,266,234.[[4]](#cite_note-popest-4) Nearly 10% of Saskatchewan's total area of 651,900 square kilometres (251,700 sq mi) is fresh water, mostly rivers, reservoirs, and [lakes](/wiki/List_of_lakes_in_Saskatchewan "List of lakes in Saskatchewan").

Saskatchewanians live primarily in the southern prairie half of the province, while the northern half is mostly forested and sparsely populated. Roughly half live in Saskatchewan's two largest cities; [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan") (the province's capital city) and [Saskatoon](/wiki/Saskatoon "Saskatoon") (the province's largest city). Other notable cities in Saskatchewan include [Prince Albert](/wiki/Prince_Albert,_Saskatchewan "Prince Albert, Saskatchewan"), [Moose Jaw](/wiki/Moose_Jaw "Moose Jaw"), [Yorkton](/wiki/Yorkton "Yorkton"), [Swift Current](/wiki/Swift_Current "Swift Current"), [North Battleford](/wiki/North_Battleford "North Battleford"), [Estevan](/wiki/Estevan "Estevan"), [Weyburn](/wiki/Weyburn "Weyburn"), [Melfort](/wiki/Melfort,_Saskatchewan "Melfort, Saskatchewan"), and the border city; [Lloydminster](/wiki/Lloydminster "Lloydminster").[[10]](#cite_note-StatsCan09-11) [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is the primary language of the province, with 82.4% of Saskatchewanians speaking English as their [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Saskatchewan has been inhabited for thousands of years by [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada"). Europeans first explored any part of the province in 1690 and first settled in the area in 1774. It became a province in 1905, carved out from the vast [North-West Territories](/wiki/History_of_the_Northwest_Territories "History of the Northwest Territories"), which had until then included most of the [Canadian Prairies](/wiki/Canadian_Prairies "Canadian Prairies"). In the early 20th century, the province became known as a stronghold for Canadian social democracy, with the [1944 provincial election](/wiki/1944_Saskatchewan_general_election "1944 Saskatchewan general election") electing [North America's first socialist government](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Co-operative Commonwealth Federation") to office.

Saskatchewan's economy is based on [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining"), and [energy](/wiki/Energy_industry "Energy industry"). In 1992, the federal and provincial governments signed a historic land claim agreement with [First Nations in Saskatchewan](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Saskatchewan "First Nations in Saskatchewan"), granting these nations compensation which they could use to buy land on the open market for the bands.[[12]](#cite_note-aadnc-aandc1-13) Presently, Saskatchewan is governed by the [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party"), led by Premier [Scott Moe](/wiki/Scott_Moe "Scott Moe"), which has been in power since 2007.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name of the province, "Saskatchewan", is derived from the [Saskatchewan River](/wiki/Saskatchewan_River "Saskatchewan River"). The river is known as ᑭᓯᐢᑳᒋᐘᓂ ᓰᐱᐩ *kisiskāciwani-sīpiy* ("swift flowing river") in the [Cree language](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language").[[13]](#cite_note-name-14) [Anthony Henday's](/wiki/Anthony_Henday "Anthony Henday") spelling was *Keiskatchewan*, with the modern rendering, *Saskatchewan*, being officially adopted in 1882, when a portion of the present-day province was designated a [provisional district of the North-West Territories](/wiki/Provisional_district_of_the_North-West_Territories "Provisional district of the North-West Territories").[[14]](#cite_note-15)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Geography_of_Saskatchewan "Geography of Saskatchewan")

See also: [List of regions of Saskatchewan](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_Saskatchewan "List of regions of Saskatchewan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Saskatchewan_Topographic.png/250px-Saskatchewan_Topographic.png)](/wiki/File:Saskatchewan_Topographic.png)

A topographic map of Saskatchewan, showing cities, towns, rural municipality borders, and natural features.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Highest_point_in_Saskatchewan%2C_looking_south%2C_2025_01.jpg/250px-Highest_point_in_Saskatchewan%2C_looking_south%2C_2025_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Highest_point_in_Saskatchewan,_looking_south,_2025_01.jpg)

The highest point in Saskatchewan, at 1,392 metres (4,567 ft), is found near the [Cypress Hills](/wiki/Cypress_Hills_(Canada) "Cypress Hills (Canada)").

Saskatchewan is the only province without a [natural border](/wiki/Natural_border "Natural border"). As its borders follow geographic lines of [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") and [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), the province is roughly a [quadrilateral](/wiki/Quadrilateral "Quadrilateral"), or a shape with four sides. However, the southern border on the [49th parallel](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north") and the northern border on the [60th parallel](/wiki/60th_parallel_north "60th parallel north") curve to the left as one proceeds east, as do all parallels in the Northern Hemisphere. Additionally, the eastern boundary of the province follows range lines and [correction lines](/wiki/Correction_line "Correction line") of the [Dominion Land Survey](/wiki/Dominion_Land_Survey "Dominion Land Survey"), laid out by surveyors before the *[Dominion Lands Act](/wiki/Dominion_Lands_Act "Dominion Lands Act")* [homestead](/wiki/Homestead_principle "Homestead principle") program (1880–1928).

Saskatchewan is bounded on the west by [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), on the north by the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"), on the north-east by [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut"), on the east by [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), and on the south by the [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") and [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"). Saskatchewan has the distinction of being the only Canadian province for which no borders correspond to physical geographic features (i.e., they are all parallels and meridians). Along with Alberta, Saskatchewan is one of only two [land-locked](/wiki/Land-locked "Land-locked") provinces.

The overwhelming majority of Saskatchewan's population is in the southern third of the province, south of the [53rd parallel](/wiki/53rd_parallel_north "53rd parallel north").

Saskatchewan contains two major natural regions: the [boreal forest](/wiki/Boreal_Forest_of_Canada "Boreal Forest of Canada") in the north and the [prairies](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie") in the south. They are separated by an [aspen parkland](/wiki/Aspen_parkland "Aspen parkland") transition zone near the [North Saskatchewan River](/wiki/North_Saskatchewan_River "North Saskatchewan River") on the western side of the province, and near to south of the [Saskatchewan River](/wiki/Saskatchewan_River "Saskatchewan River") on the eastern side. Northern Saskatchewan is mostly covered by forest except for the [Lake Athabasca Sand Dunes](/wiki/Lake_Athabasca_Sand_Dunes "Lake Athabasca Sand Dunes"), the largest active sand dunes in the world north of 58°, and adjacent to the southern shore of [Lake Athabasca](/wiki/Lake_Athabasca "Lake Athabasca"). Southern Saskatchewan contains another area with sand dunes known as the "[Great Sand Hills](/wiki/Great_Sand_Hills "Great Sand Hills")", covering over 300 square kilometres (120 sq mi). The [Cypress Hills](/wiki/Cypress_Hills_(Canada) "Cypress Hills (Canada)"), in the southwestern corner of Saskatchewan and Killdeer Badlands ([Grasslands National Park](/wiki/Grasslands_National_Park "Grasslands National Park")), are areas of the province that were unglaciated during the last glaciation period, the [Wisconsin glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_glaciation "Wisconsin glaciation").

The province's highest point, at 1,392 metres (4,567 ft), is in the [Cypress Hills](/wiki/Cypress_Hills_(Canada) "Cypress Hills (Canada)") less than two kilometres (1.2 mi) from the provincial boundary with Alberta.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) The lowest point is the shore of [Lake Athabasca](/wiki/Lake_Athabasca "Lake Athabasca"), at 213 metres (699 ft). The province has 14 major [drainage basins](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") made up of various rivers and watersheds draining into the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean"), [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay"), and the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Saskatchewan_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Saskatchewan_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Saskatchewan_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Saskatchewan

Saskatchewan receives more hours of [sunshine](/wiki/Sunshine "Sunshine") than any other Canadian province.[[18]](#cite_note-19) The province lies far from any significant body of water. This fact, combined with its northerly latitude, gives it a warm summer, corresponding to its [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen type *Dfb*](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")) in the central and most of the eastern parts of the province, as well as the [Cypress Hills](/wiki/Cypress_Hills_(Canada) "Cypress Hills (Canada)"); drying off to a [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid "Semi-arid") steppe climate (Köppen type *BSk*) in the southwestern part of the province. Drought can affect agricultural areas during long periods with little or no precipitation at all. The northern parts of Saskatchewan – from about [La Ronge](/wiki/La_Ronge "La Ronge") northward – have a [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") (Köppen *Dfc*) with a shorter summer season. Summers can get very hot, sometimes above 38 °C (100 °F) during the day, and with humidity decreasing from northeast to southwest. Warm southern winds blow from the plains and intermontane regions of the Western United States during much of July and August, and very cool or hot but changeable air masses often occur during spring and in September. Winters are usually bitterly cold, with frequent Arctic air descending from the north,[[19]](#cite_note-20) and with high temperatures not breaking −17 °C (1 °F) for weeks at a time. Warm [chinook winds](/wiki/Chinook_winds "Chinook winds") often blow from the west, bringing periods of mild weather. Annual precipitation averages 30 to 45 centimetres (12 to 18 inches) across the province, with the bulk of rain falling in June, July, and August.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Saskatchewan is one of the most [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado")-active parts of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), averaging roughly 12 to 18 tornadoes per year, some violent. In 2012, 33 tornadoes were reported in the province. The [Regina Cyclone](/wiki/Regina_Cyclone "Regina Cyclone") took place in June 1912 when 28 people died in an F4 [Fujita scale](/wiki/Fujita_scale "Fujita scale") tornado. Severe and non-severe thunderstorm events occur in Saskatchewan, usually from early spring to late summer. Hail, strong winds and isolated tornadoes are a common occurrence.

The hottest temperature ever recorded in Saskatchewan was in July 1937 when the temperature rose to 45 °C (113 °F) in [Midale](/wiki/Midale "Midale") and [Yellow Grass](/wiki/Yellow_Grass "Yellow Grass"). The coldest ever recorded in the province was −56.7 °C (−70.1 °F) in [Prince Albert](/wiki/Prince_Albert,_Saskatchewan "Prince Albert, Saskatchewan"), north of Saskatoon, in February 1893.

#### Climate change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate change")]

Main article: [Climate change in Saskatchewan](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Saskatchewan "Climate change in Saskatchewan")

The effects of [climate change in Saskatchewan](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Saskatchewan "Climate change in Saskatchewan") are now being observed in parts of the province. Evidence of reduction of [biomass](/wiki/Biomass_(ecology) "Biomass (ecology)") in Saskatchewan's [boreal forests](/wiki/Boreal_forests "Boreal forests")[[22]](#cite_note-23) (as with those of other [Canadian prairie provinces](/wiki/Prairie_provinces "Prairie provinces")) is linked by researchers to drought-related water stress, stemming from [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming"), most likely caused by [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"). While studies as early as 1988 (Williams, et al., 1988) have shown climate change will affect agriculture,[[23]](#cite_note-24) whether the effects can be mitigated through adaptations of [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivars "Cultivars"), or crops, is less clear. [Resiliency](/wiki/Resilience_(ecology) "Resilience (ecology)") of [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") may decline with large changes in temperature.[[24]](#cite_note-25) The provincial government has responded to the threat of climate change by introducing a plan to reduce [carbon emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas"), "The Saskatchewan Energy and Climate Change Plan", in June 2007.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Saskatchewan](/wiki/History_of_Saskatchewan "History of Saskatchewan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Henry_Kelsey_sees_the_buffalo_on_the_western_plains.jpg/250px-Henry_Kelsey_sees_the_buffalo_on_the_western_plains.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry_Kelsey_sees_the_buffalo_on_the_western_plains.jpg)

[Charles William Jefferys](/wiki/Charles_William_Jefferys "Charles William Jefferys")'s 20th century illustration of [Henry Kelsey](/wiki/Henry_Kelsey "Henry Kelsey") observing a herd of [bison](/wiki/American_bison "American bison") on the western plains.

Saskatchewan has been populated by various [indigenous peoples of North America](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_North_America "Indigenous peoples of North America"), including members of the [Sarcee](/wiki/Sarcee_people "Sarcee people"), [Niitsitapi](/wiki/Niitsitapi "Niitsitapi"), [Atsina](/wiki/Gros_Ventre_people "Gros Ventre people"), [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree"), [Saulteaux](/wiki/Saulteaux "Saulteaux"), [Assiniboine](/wiki/Assiniboine_people "Assiniboine people") (Nakoda), and [Sioux](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux").

The first known European to enter Saskatchewan was [Henry Kelsey](/wiki/Henry_Kelsey "Henry Kelsey") from England in 1690, who travelled up the Saskatchewan River in hopes of trading fur with the region's indigenous peoples. [Fort La Jonquière](/wiki/Fort_La_Jonqui%C3%A8re "Fort La Jonquière") and [Fort de la Corne](/wiki/Fort_de_la_Corne "Fort de la Corne") were first established in 1751 and 1753 by early French explorers and traders. The first permanent European settlement was a [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") post at [Cumberland House](/wiki/Cumberland_House,_Saskatchewan "Cumberland House, Saskatchewan"), founded in 1774 by [Samuel Hearne](/wiki/Samuel_Hearne "Samuel Hearne").[[25]](#cite_note-26) The southern part of the province was part of [Spanish Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)") from 1762 until 1802.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 19th century")]

In 1803, the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") transferred from France to the United States part of what is now [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and Saskatchewan. In 1818, the U.S. ceded the area to Britain. Most of what is now Saskatchewan was part of [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land") and controlled by the Hudson's Bay Company, which claimed rights to all watersheds flowing into [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay"), including the [Saskatchewan River](/wiki/Saskatchewan_River "Saskatchewan River"), [Churchill](/wiki/Churchill_River_(Hudson_Bay) "Churchill River (Hudson Bay)"), [Assiniboine](/wiki/Assiniboine_River "Assiniboine River"), [Souris](/wiki/Souris_River "Souris River"), and [Qu'Appelle River](/wiki/Qu%27Appelle_River "Qu'Appelle River") systems.

In the late 1850s and early 1860s, scientific expeditions led by [John Palliser](/wiki/John_Palliser "John Palliser") and [Henry Youle Hind](/wiki/Henry_Youle_Hind "Henry Youle Hind") explored the prairie region of the province.

In 1870, Canada acquired the Hudson's Bay Company's territories and formed the [North-West Territories](/wiki/North-West_Territories "North-West Territories") to administer the vast territory between [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") and [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"). The Crown also entered into a series of [numbered treaties](/wiki/Numbered_Treaties "Numbered Treaties") with the indigenous peoples of the area, which serve as the basis of the relationship between [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"), as they are called today, and the Crown. Since the late twentieth century, land losses and inequities as a result of those treaties have been subject to negotiation for settlement between the [First Nations in Saskatchewan](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Saskatchewan "First Nations in Saskatchewan") and the federal government, in collaboration with provincial governments.

In 1876, following their defeat of United States Army forces at the [Battle of the Little Bighorn](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Little_Bighorn "Battle of the Little Bighorn") in [Montana Territory](/wiki/Montana_Territory "Montana Territory") in the United States, the [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people") Chief [Sitting Bull](/wiki/Sitting_Bull "Sitting Bull") led several thousand of his people to [Wood Mountain](/wiki/Wood_Mountain,_Saskatchewan "Wood Mountain, Saskatchewan"). Survivors and descendants founded Wood Mountain Reserve in 1914.

The North-West Mounted Police set up several posts and forts across Saskatchewan, including [Fort Walsh](/wiki/Fort_Walsh "Fort Walsh") in the Cypress Hills, and [Wood Mountain](/wiki/Wood_Mountain_Regional_Park "Wood Mountain Regional Park") Post in south-central Saskatchewan near the United States border.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Battle_of_Batoche_Print_by_Seargent_Grundy.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Batoche_Print_by_Seargent_Grundy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Batoche_Print_by_Seargent_Grundy.jpg)

The 1885 [Battle of Batoche](/wiki/Battle_of_Batoche "Battle of Batoche") was a battle during the [North-West Rebellion](/wiki/North-West_Rebellion "North-West Rebellion"). 1885 illustration by Sergeant Grundy

Many [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_(people) "Métis (people)") people, who had not been signatories to a treaty, had moved to the [Southbranch Settlement](/wiki/Southbranch_Settlement "Southbranch Settlement") and [Prince Albert](/wiki/Prince_Albert,_Saskatchewan "Prince Albert, Saskatchewan") district north of present-day Saskatoon following the [Red River Rebellion](/wiki/Red_River_Rebellion "Red River Rebellion") in Manitoba in 1870. In the early 1880s, the Canadian government refused to hear the Métis' grievances, which stemmed from land-use issues. Finally, in 1885, the Métis, led by [Louis Riel](/wiki/Louis_Riel "Louis Riel"), staged the [North-West Rebellion](/wiki/North-West_Rebellion "North-West Rebellion") and declared a provisional government. They were defeated by a Canadian militia brought to the [Canadian prairies](/wiki/Canadian_prairies "Canadian prairies") by the new Canadian Pacific Railway. Riel, who surrendered and was convicted of treason in a packed Regina courtroom, was hanged on November 16, 1885. Since then, the government has recognized the Métis as an aboriginal people with status rights and provided them with various benefits.

#### European settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: European settlements")]

The national policy set by the federal government, the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway"), the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") and associated land companies encouraged immigration. The *[Dominion Lands Act](/wiki/Dominion_Lands_Act "Dominion Lands Act")* of 1872 permitted settlers to acquire one-quarter of a square mile of land to homestead and offered an additional quarter upon establishing a homestead. In 1874, the North-West Mounted Police began providing police services. In 1876, the *North-West Territories Act* provided for appointment, by Ottawa, of a Lieutenant Governor and a Council to assist him.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Ad_to_attract_Immigrants_to_wheat_belt_in_1898.jpg/250px-Ad_to_attract_Immigrants_to_wheat_belt_in_1898.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ad_to_attract_Immigrants_to_wheat_belt_in_1898.jpg)

An ad to attract immigrants to [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada"), 1898

Highly optimistic advertising campaigns promoted the benefits of prairie living. Potential immigrants read leaflets that described Canada as a favourable place to live and downplayed the need for agricultural expertise. Ads in *The Nor'-West Farmer* by the Commissioner of Immigration implied that western land held water, wood, gold, silver, iron, copper, and cheap coal for fuel, all of which were readily at hand. The reality was far harsher, especially for the first arrivals who lived in [sod houses](/wiki/Sod_house "Sod house"). However eastern money poured in and by 1913, long term mortgage loans to Saskatchewan farmers had reached $65 million.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The dominant groups comprised British settlers from eastern Canada and Britain, who comprised about half of the population during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. They played the leading role in establishing the basic institutions of plains society, economy and government.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Gender roles were sharply defined. Men were primarily responsible for breaking the land; planting and harvesting; building the house; buying, operating and repairing machinery; and handling finances. At first, there were many single men on the prairie, or husbands whose wives were still back east, but they had a hard time. They realized the need for a wife. In 1901, there were 19,200 families, but this surged to 150,300 families only 15 years later. Wives played a central role in settlement of the prairie region. Their labour, skills, and ability to adapt to the harsh environment proved decisive in meeting the challenges. They prepared bannock, beans and bacon, mended clothes, raised children, cleaned, tended the garden, helped at harvest time and nursed everyone back to health. While prevailing patriarchal attitudes, legislation, and economic principles obscured women's contributions, the flexibility exhibited by farm women in performing productive and nonproductive labour was critical to the survival of family farms, and thus to the success of the wheat economy.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Banquet_celebrating_new_province_of_Saskatchewan.jpg/250px-Banquet_celebrating_new_province_of_Saskatchewan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banquet_celebrating_new_province_of_Saskatchewan.jpg)

A banquet being held to commemorate the creation of Saskatchewan, 1905

On September 1, 1905, Saskatchewan became a province, with inauguration day held on September 4. Its political leaders at the time proclaimed its destiny was to become Canada's most powerful province. Saskatchewan embarked on an ambitious province-building program based on its Anglo-Canadian culture and wheat production for the export market. Population quintupled from 91,000 in 1901 to 492,000 in 1911, thanks to heavy immigration of farmers from Ukraine, U.S., Germany and Scandinavia. Efforts were made to assimilate the newcomers to British Canadian culture and values.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

In the 1905 provincial elections, Liberals won 16 of 25 seats in Saskatchewan. The Saskatchewan government bought out Bell Telephone Company in 1909, with the government owning the long-distance lines and left local service to small companies organized at the municipal level.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Premier Walter Scott preferred government assistance to outright ownership because he thought enterprises worked better if citizens had a stake in running them; he set up the Saskatchewan Cooperative Elevator Company in 1911. Despite pressure from farm groups for direct government involvement in the grain handling business, the Scott government opted to loan money to a farmer-owned elevator company. Saskatchewan in 1909 provided bond guarantees to railway companies for the construction of branch lines, alleviating the concerns of farmers who had trouble getting their wheat to market by waggon.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The [Saskatchewan Grain Growers Association](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Grain_Growers_Association "Saskatchewan Grain Growers Association"), was the dominant political force in the province until the 1920s; it had close ties with the governing Liberal party. In 1913, the Saskatchewan Stock Growers Association was established with three goals: to watch over legislation; to forward the interests of the stock growers in every honourable and legitimate way; and to suggest to parliament legislation to meet changing conditions and requirements.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Maury_Geography_073A_Saskatchewan.jpg/250px-Maury_Geography_073A_Saskatchewan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maury_Geography_073A_Saskatchewan.jpg)

Farmers at work in 1907. The introduction of [Marquis wheat](/wiki/Marquis_wheat "Marquis wheat") saw wheat output soar in the province.

Immigration peaked in 1910, and in spite of the initial difficulties of frontier life – distance from towns, sod homes, and backbreaking labour – new settlers established a European-Canadian style of prosperous [agrarian society](/wiki/Agrarian_society "Agrarian society"). The long-term prosperity of the province depended on the world price of grain, which headed steadily upward from the 1880s to 1920, then plunged down. Wheat output was increased by new strains, such as the "[Marquis wheat](/wiki/Marquis_wheat "Marquis wheat")" strain which matured 8 days sooner and yielded 7 more bushels per acre (0.72 m3/ha) than the previous standard, "[Red Fife](/wiki/Red_Fife "Red Fife")". The national output of wheat soared from 8 million [imperial bushels](/wiki/Imperial_bushel "Imperial bushel") (290,000 m3) in 1896, to 26×10~~^~~6 imp bu (950,000 m3) in 1901, reaching 151×10~~^~~6 imp bu (5,500,000 m3) by 1921.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Urban reform movements in Regina were based on support from business and professional groups. City planning, reform of local government, and municipal ownership of utilities were more widely supported by these two groups, often through such organizations as the Board of Trade. Church-related and other altruistic organizations generally supported social welfare and housing reforms; these groups were generally less successful in getting their own reforms enacted.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

The province responded to the First World War in 1914 with patriotic enthusiasm and enjoyed the resultant economic boom for farms and cities alike. Emotional and intellectual support for the war emerged from the politics of Canadian national identity, the rural myth, and social gospel progressivism The Church of England was especially supportive. However, there was strong hostility toward German-Canadian farmers.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Recent Ukrainian immigrants were enemy aliens because of their citizenship in the Austro-Hungarian Empire. A small fraction were [taken to internment camps.](/wiki/Ukrainian_Canadian_internment "Ukrainian Canadian internment") Most of the internees were unskilled unemployed labourers who were imprisoned "because they were destitute, not because they were disloyal".[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Hawker-Eaton-InternmentCamp.jpg/250px-Hawker-Eaton-InternmentCamp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hawker-Eaton-InternmentCamp.jpg)

A memorial stone for [Ukrainian Canadians interned](/wiki/Ukrainian_Canadian_internment "Ukrainian Canadian internment") during the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") at the [Saskatchewan Railway Museum](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Railway_Museum "Saskatchewan Railway Museum")

The price of wheat tripled and acreage seeded doubled. The wartime spirit of sacrifice intensified social reform movements that had predated the war and now came to fruition. Saskatchewan gave women the right to vote in 1916 and at the end of 1916 passed a referendum to prohibit the sale of alcohol.

In the late 1920s, the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan_in_Canada "Ku Klux Klan in Canada"), imported from the United States and Ontario, gained brief popularity in nativist circles in Saskatchewan and Alberta. The Klan, briefly allied with the provincial Conservative party because of their mutual dislike for Premier [James G. "Jimmy" Gardiner](/wiki/James_G._Gardiner "James G. Gardiner") and his Liberals (who ferociously fought the Klan), enjoyed about two years of prominence. It declined and disappeared, subject to widespread political and media opposition, plus internal scandals involving the use of the organization's funds.

#### Post–Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post–Second World War")]

In 1970, the first annual [Canadian Western Agribition](/wiki/Canadian_Western_Agribition "Canadian Western Agribition") was held in Regina. This farm-industry trade show, with its strong emphasis on livestock, is rated as one of the five top livestock shows in North America, along with those in [Houston](/wiki/Houston,_Texas "Houston, Texas"), [Denver](/wiki/Denver,_Colorado "Denver, Colorado"), [Louisville](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky") and [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Queen_and_burmese.jpg/250px-Queen_and_burmese.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_and_burmese.jpg)

An equestrian statue of [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") in [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan"). The statue was unveiled by the Queen in 2005.

The province celebrated the 75th anniversary of its establishment in 1980, with [Princess Margaret, Countess of Snowdon](/wiki/Princess_Margaret,_Countess_of_Snowdon "Princess Margaret, Countess of Snowdon"), presiding over the official ceremonies.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) In 2005, 25 years later, her sister, Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), attended the events held to mark Saskatchewan's centennial.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Since the late 20th century, First Nations have become more politically active in seeking justice for past inequities, especially related to the taking of indigenous lands by various governments. The federal and provincial governments have negotiated on numerous land claims, and developed a program of "Treaty Land Entitlement", enabling First Nations to buy land to be taken into reserves with money from settlements of claims.

> "In 1992, the federal and provincial governments signed an historic land claim agreement with Saskatchewan First Nations. Under the Agreement, the First Nations received money to buy land on the open market. As a result, about 761,000 acres have been turned into reserve land and many First Nations continue to invest their settlement dollars in urban areas", including Saskatoon. The money from such settlements has enabled First Nations to invest in businesses and other economic infrastructure.[[12]](#cite_note-aadnc-aandc1-13)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In June 2021, a graveyard containing the remains of 751 unidentified people was found at the former [Marieval Indian Residential School](/wiki/Marieval_Indian_Residential_School "Marieval Indian Residential School"), part of the [Canadian Indian residential school system](/wiki/Canadian_Indian_residential_school_system "Canadian Indian residential school system").[[44]](#cite_note-45)

### Ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]

According to the [2011 Canadian census](/wiki/2011_Canadian_census "2011 Canadian census"), the largest [ethnic group](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") in Saskatchewan is [German](/wiki/German_people "German people") (28.6%), followed by [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") (24.9%), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") (18.9%), [Canadian](/wiki/Canadian_ethnicity "Canadian ethnicity") (18.8%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") (15.5%), [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_people "Ukrainian people") (13.5%), [French](/wiki/French_people "French people") ([Fransaskois](/wiki/Fransaskois "Fransaskois")) (12.2%), [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") (12.1%), [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_people "Norwegian people") (6.9%), and [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") (5.8%).[[45]](#cite_note-46)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Language")]

As of the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), the ten most spoken languages in the province included [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") (1,094,785 or 99.24%), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (52,065 or 4.72%), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") (36,125 or 3.27%), [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language") (24,850 or 2.25%), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") (15,745 or 1.43%), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (13,310 or 1.21%), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") (11,815 or 1.07%), [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (11,590 or 1.05%), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") (11,185 or 1.01%), and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") (10,795 or 0.98%).[[46]](#cite_note-language2021-47) The question on knowledge of languages allows for multiple responses.

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Religion")]

According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), religious groups in Saskatchewan included:[[47]](#cite_note-2021censusB-48)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Economy_of_Saskatchewan "Economy of Saskatchewan")

Historically, Saskatchewan's economy was primarily associated with [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), with wheat being the precious symbol on the province's flag. Increasing diversification has resulted in agriculture, [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"), [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing"), and [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting") only making up 8.9% of the province's GDP in 2018. Saskatchewan grows a large portion of Canada's grain.[[48]](#cite_note-49) In 2017, the production of [canola](/wiki/Rapeseed "Rapeseed") surpassed the production of [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), which is Saskatchewan's most familiar crop and the one most often associated with the province. The total net income from farming was $3.3 billion in 2017, which was $0.9 billion less than the income in 2016.[[49]](#cite_note-50) Other grains such as [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax"), [rye](/wiki/Rye "Rye"), [oats](/wiki/Oat "Oat"), [peas](/wiki/Pea "Pea"), [lentils](/wiki/Lentil "Lentil"), canary seed, and [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley") are also produced in the province. Saskatchewan is the world's largest exporter of mustard seed.[[50]](#cite_note-51) [Beef cattle](/wiki/Beef_cattle "Beef cattle") production by a Canadian province is only exceeded by Alberta. In the northern part of the province, forestry is also a significant industry.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/PotashCorporationOfSaskatchewanPatienceLakeMine.jpg/250px-PotashCorporationOfSaskatchewanPatienceLakeMine.jpg)](/wiki/File:PotashCorporationOfSaskatchewanPatienceLakeMine.jpg)

[Mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") is a major industry in the province, with Saskatchewan being the world's largest exporter of [potash](/wiki/Potash "Potash") and [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium").[[54]](#cite_note-55) [Oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") production is also a very important part of Saskatchewan's economy, although the [oil industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry") is larger. Among Canadian provinces, only Alberta exceeds Saskatchewan in overall oil production.[[55]](#cite_note-56) Heavy crude is extracted in the Lloydminster-Kerrobert-Kindersley areas. Light crude is found in the Kindersley-Swift Current areas as well as the Weyburn-Estevan fields. Natural gas is found almost entirely in the western part of Saskatchewan, from the [Primrose Lake](/wiki/Primrose_Lake "Primrose Lake") area through Lloydminster, Unity, Kindersley, Leader, and around Maple Creek areas.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Major companies based in Saskatchewan include [Nutrien](/wiki/Nutrien "Nutrien"), [Federated Cooperatives Ltd.](/wiki/Federated_Co-operatives "Federated Co-operatives") and [Cameco](/wiki/Cameco "Cameco").[[57]](#cite_note-58)

Major Saskatchewan-based [Crown corporations](/wiki/Crown_corporations "Crown corporations") are [Saskatchewan Government Insurance](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Government_Insurance "Saskatchewan Government Insurance") (SGI), [SaskTel](/wiki/SaskTel "SaskTel"), [SaskEnergy](/wiki/SaskEnergy "SaskEnergy") (the province's main supplier of natural gas), [SaskPower](/wiki/SaskPower "SaskPower"), and Saskatchewan Crop Insurance Corporation (SCIC). [Bombardier](/wiki/Bombardier_Aerospace "Bombardier Aerospace") runs the NATO Flying Training Centre at 15 Wing, near [Moose Jaw](/wiki/Moose_Jaw "Moose Jaw"). Bombardier was awarded a long-term contract in the late 1990s for $2.8 billion from the [federal government](/wiki/Politics_of_Canada "Politics of Canada") for the purchase of military aircraft and the running of the training facility. [SaskPower](/wiki/SaskPower "SaskPower") since 1929 has been the principal supplier of electricity in Saskatchewan, serving more than 451,000 customers and managing $4.5 billion in assets. SaskPower is a major employer in the province with almost 2,500 permanent full-time staff in 71 communities.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Saskatchewan](/wiki/Education_in_Saskatchewan "Education in Saskatchewan")

Publicly funded elementary and secondary schools in the province are administered by [twenty-seven school divisions](/wiki/List_of_Saskatchewan_school_divisions "List of Saskatchewan school divisions"). Public elementary and secondary schools either operate as [secular](/wiki/Secular_education "Secular education") or as a [separate schools](/wiki/Separate_school "Separate school"). Nearly all school divisions, except one operate as an English [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language") school board. The Division scolaire francophone No. 310 is the only school division that operates French first language schools. In addition to elementary and secondary schools, the province is also home to several post-secondary institutions.

The first education on the prairies took place within the family groups of the First Nations and early [fur trading](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") settlers. There were only a few missionary or trading post schools established in [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land") – later known as the [North West Territories](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_Canada "Territorial evolution of Canada"). The first 76 [North-West Territories](/wiki/North-West_Territories "North-West Territories") school districts and the first Board of Education meeting formed in 1886. The pioneering boom formed [ethnic bloc settlements](/wiki/Block_Settlement "Block Settlement"). Communities were seeking education for their children similar to the schools of their homeland. [Log cabins](/wiki/Log_cabin "Log cabin"), and dwellings were constructed for the assembly of the community, school, church, dances and meetings.

The prosperity of the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties") and the success of farmers in proving up on their homesteads helped provide funding to standardize education.[[58]](#cite_note-59) Textbooks, normal schools for educating teachers, formal school curricula and schoolhouse [architectural plans](/wiki/Architectural_plan "Architectural plan") provided continuity throughout the province. English as the school language helped to provide economic stability because one community could communicate with another and goods could be traded and sold in a common language. The number of one-room schoolhouse districts across Saskatchewan totalled approximately 5,000 at the height of this system of education in the late 1940s.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

Following World War II, the transition from many one-room schoolhouses to fewer and larger consolidated modern technological town and city schools occurred as a means of ensuring technical education. School buses, highways, and family vehicles create ease and accessibility of a population shift to larger towns and cities. Combines and tractors mean the farmer could manage more than a quarter section of land, so there was a shift from [family farms](/wiki/Family_farm "Family farm") and [subsistence crops](/wiki/Subsistence_crops "Subsistence crops") to [cash crops](/wiki/Cash_crops "Cash crops") grown on many sections of land. [School vouchers](/wiki/School_vouchers "School vouchers") have been newly proposed as a means of allowing competition between rural schools and making the operation of [cooperative schools](/wiki/Cooperative_school "Cooperative school") practicable in rural areas.

## Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Royal_University_Hospital.jpg/250px-Royal_University_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_University_Hospital.jpg)

The [Royal University Hospital](/wiki/Royal_University_Hospital "Royal University Hospital") is one of [several hospitals operating in Saskatchewan](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Saskatchewan "List of hospitals in Saskatchewan").

Saskatchewan's [Ministry of Health](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_(Saskatchewan) "Ministry of Health (Saskatchewan)") is responsible for policy direction, sets and monitors standards, and provides funding for regional health authorities and provincial health services. Saskatchewan's health system is a [single-payer system](/wiki/Single_payer_health_care "Single payer health care"). Medical practitioners in Saskatchewan are independent contractors. They remit their accounts to the publicly funded Saskatchewan Medical Care Insurance Plan, which pays the accounts. Patients do not pay anything to their doctors or hospitals for medical care.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

In 1944, the [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Co-operative Commonwealth Federation") (CCF), a left-wing agrarian and labour party, won the provincial election in Saskatchewan and formed the first socialist government in North American history. Repeatedly re-elected, the CCF campaigned in the early 1960s on the theme of [universal health coverage](/wiki/Universal_health_coverage "Universal health coverage") and, after winning the election again, implemented it, the first in Canada. However, it was fiercely opposed by the province's doctors' union, which went on a massive strike the day the new system came into effect. Supported by the Saskatchewan Chamber of Commerce, most newspapers and the right-wing Keep Our Doctors movement, the doctors' union ran an effective communications campaign portraying the universal health care system as a communist scheme that would spread disease. The strike, which had become very unpopular because of the outrageous rhetoric of some of its leaders (one of them had called for bloodshed), finally ended after a few weeks, and universal health coverage was adopted by the whole country five years later.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Politics of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Politics_of_Saskatchewan "Politics of Saskatchewan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Saskatchewan_Legislative_Building_Front.JPG/250px-Saskatchewan_Legislative_Building_Front.JPG)](/wiki/File:Saskatchewan_Legislative_Building_Front.JPG)

The [Saskatchewan Legislative Building](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Legislative_Building "Saskatchewan Legislative Building") is the meeting place for the province's [legislative assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan").

Saskatchewan has the same form of government[[62]](#cite_note-63) as the other Canadian provinces with a [lieutenant-governor](/wiki/Lieutenant-Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant-Governor (Canada)") (who is the representative of the [King in Right of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Monarchy_in_Saskatchewan "Monarchy in Saskatchewan")), [premier](/wiki/Premier "Premier"), and a unicameral [legislature](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature").[[63]](#cite_note-64)

During the 20th century, Saskatchewan was one of Canada's more left-wing provinces, reflecting the slant of its many rural citizens which distrusted the distant capital government and which favoured a strong local government to attend to their issues. In 1944 [Tommy Douglas](/wiki/Tommy_Douglas "Tommy Douglas") became premier of the first avowedly [socialist](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") regional government in North America. Most of his [Members of the Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Members_of_the_Legislative_Assembly "Members of the Legislative Assembly") (MLAs) represented rural and small-town ridings. Under his [Cooperative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Cooperative_Commonwealth_Federation "Cooperative Commonwealth Federation") government, Saskatchewan became the first province to have [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(Canada) "Medicare (Canada)"). In 1961, Douglas left provincial politics to become the first leader of the federal [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_(Canada) "New Democratic Party (Canada)"). In the 21st century, Saskatchewan began to drift to the right-wing, generally attributed to the [province's economy shifting toward oil and gas production](/wiki/Petroleum_industry_in_Canada#Saskatchewan "Petroleum industry in Canada"). In the [2015 federal election](/wiki/2015_Canadian_federal_election "2015 Canadian federal election"), the [Conservative Party of Canada](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") won ten of the province's fourteen seats, followed by the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party "New Democratic Party") with three and the [Liberal Party of Canada](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") with one; in the [2019 election](/wiki/2019_Canadian_federal_election "2019 Canadian federal election"), the Conservatives won in all of Saskatchewan's 14 seats, sweeping their competition, and retained them all in the [2021 election](/wiki/2021_Canadian_federal_election "2021 Canadian federal election"); in the [2025 Canadian federal election](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_election "2025 Canadian federal election"), the Liberal Party won a single seat in northern Saskatchewan .

Provincial politics in Saskatchewan is dominated by the [social-democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") [Saskatchewan New Democratic Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") and the centre-right [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party"), with the latter holding the majority in the [Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") since [2007](/wiki/2007_Saskatchewan_general_election "2007 Saskatchewan general election"). The current [Premier of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") is [Scott Moe](/wiki/Scott_Moe "Scott Moe"), who took over the leadership of the Saskatchewan Party in [2018](/wiki/2018_Saskatchewan_Party_leadership_election "2018 Saskatchewan Party leadership election") following the resignation of [Brad Wall](/wiki/Brad_Wall "Brad Wall"). Numerous smaller political parties also run candidates in provincial elections, including the [Green Party of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Green Party of Saskatchewan"), [Buffalo Party of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Buffalo_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Buffalo Party of Saskatchewan"), [Saskatchewan Progress Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Progress_Party "Saskatchewan Progress Party"), and the [Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan"), but none is currently represented in the Legislative Assembly.

No [Prime Minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") has been born in Saskatchewan, but two ([William Lyon Mackenzie King](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie_King "William Lyon Mackenzie King") and [John Diefenbaker](/wiki/John_Diefenbaker "John Diefenbaker")) represented the province in the [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") during their tenures as head of government.

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

See also: [List of rural municipalities in Saskatchewan](/wiki/List_of_rural_municipalities_in_Saskatchewan "List of rural municipalities in Saskatchewan") and [List of municipalities in Saskatchewan](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Saskatchewan "List of municipalities in Saskatchewan")

[![Map of urban, rural and northern municipalities in Saskatchewan as of 2013](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Saskatchewan_Municipalities.png/250px-Saskatchewan_Municipalities.png)](/wiki/File:Saskatchewan_Municipalities.png)

Distribution of Saskatchewan's 466 urban, 296 rural and 24 northern municipalities (2013)

Below the provincial level of government, Saskatchewan is divided into urban and rural municipalities. The Government of Saskatchewan's Ministry of Municipal Relations recognizes three general types of municipalities and seven sub-types – urban municipalities ([cities](/wiki/City "City"), [towns](/wiki/Town "Town"), [villages](/wiki/Village "Village") and [resort villages](/wiki/Resort_village "Resort village")), [rural municipalities](/wiki/Rural_municipality "Rural municipality") and northern municipalities (northern towns, northern villages and northern hamlets).[[64]](#cite_note-munitypes-65) The vast majority of the land mass of Northern Saskatchewan is within the unorganized [Northern Saskatchewan Administration District](/wiki/Northern_Saskatchewan_Administration_District "Northern Saskatchewan Administration District"). Cities are formed under the provincial authority of *The Cities Act*, which was enacted in 2002.[[65]](#cite_note-CityAct-66) Towns, villages, resort villages and rural municipalities are formed under the authority of *The Municipalities Act*, enacted in 2005.[[66]](#cite_note-MuniAct-67) The three sub-types of northern municipalities are formed under the authority of *The Northern Municipalities Act*, enacted in 2010.[[67]](#cite_note-NorthMuniAct-68)

In 2016, Saskatchewan's 774 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") covered 52.7% of the province's land mass and were home to 94.8% of its population.[[64]](#cite_note-munitypes-65)[[68]](#cite_note-2016StatCanSK-69)[[b]](#cite_note-70)

These 774 municipalities are [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") "creatures of provincial jurisdiction" with [legal personhood](/wiki/Legal_person "Legal person").[[69]](#cite_note-munisystem-71) One of the key purposes of Saskatchewan's municipalities are "to provide services, facilities and other things that, in the opinion of council, are necessary or desirable for all or a part of the municipality".[[69]](#cite_note-munisystem-71) Other purposes are to: "provide good government"; "develop and maintain a safe and viable community"; "foster economic, social and environmental well-being" and "provide wise stewardship of public assets."[[69]](#cite_note-munisystem-71)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in Saskatchewan](/wiki/Transportation_in_Saskatchewan "Transportation in Saskatchewan")

Transportation in Saskatchewan includes an infrastructure system of roads, highways, freeways, airports, ferries, pipelines, trails, waterways and railway systems [[70]](#cite_note-72) serving a population of approximately 1,003,299 (according to 2007 estimates) inhabitants year-round.[[71]](#cite_note-73) The Saskatchewan Department of Highways and Transportation estimates 80% of traffic is carried on the 5,031-kilometre principal system of highways.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/QuAppelleRiverHwy2.jpg/250px-QuAppelleRiverHwy2.jpg)](/wiki/File:QuAppelleRiverHwy2.jpg)

The [CanAm Highway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_2 "Saskatchewan Highway 2") near [Buffalo Pound Lake](/wiki/Buffalo_Pound_Lake "Buffalo Pound Lake")

The [Ministry of Highways and Infrastructure](/wiki/Ministry_of_Highways_and_Infrastructure_(Saskatchewan) "Ministry of Highways and Infrastructure (Saskatchewan)") operates over 26,000 km (16,000 mi) of highways and [divided highways](/wiki/Dual_carriageway "Dual carriageway"). There are also municipal roads which comprise different surfaces. Asphalt concrete pavements comprise almost 9,000 km (5,600 mi), granular pavement almost 5,000 km (3,100 mi), non structural or thin membrane surface TMS are close to 7,000 km (4,300 mi) and finally gravel highways make up over 5,600 km (3,500 mi) through the province. In the northern sector, [ice roads](/wiki/Ice_road "Ice road") which can only be navigated in the winter months comprise another approximately 150 km (93 mi) of travel.[[73]](#cite_note-SHT-75) In 2024, the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") provided Saskatchewan with a $6.1-million grant for [shuttle buses](/wiki/Shuttle_bus "Shuttle bus") serving remote communities.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

Saskatchewan has over 250,000 km (160,000 mi) of roads and highways, the highest length of road surface of any Canadian province.[[75]](#cite_note-raw-77) The major highways in Saskatchewan are the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_1 "Saskatchewan Highway 1"), [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_16 "Saskatchewan Highway 16") northern Trans Canada route, [Louis Riel Trail](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_11 "Saskatchewan Highway 11"), [CanAm Highway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_2 "Saskatchewan Highway 2"), [Red Coat Trail](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_13 "Saskatchewan Highway 13"), [Northern Woods and Water route](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_55 "Saskatchewan Highway 55"), and [Saskota travel route](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_9 "Saskatchewan Highway 9").

The first [Canadian transcontinental railway](/wiki/Canadian_transcontinental_railway "Canadian transcontinental railway") was constructed by the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") (CPR) between 1881 and 1885.[[76]](#cite_note-78) After the great east–west transcontinental railway was built, north–south connector branch lines were established. The 1920s saw the largest rise in rail line track as the CPR and [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway") (CNR) fell into competition to provide rail service within ten kilometres. In the 1960s there were applications for abandonment of branch lines.[[77]](#cite_note-atlas-79) Today the only two passenger rail services in the province are *[The Canadian](/wiki/The_Canadian "The Canadian")* and [Winnipeg–Churchill train](/wiki/Winnipeg%E2%80%93Churchill_train "Winnipeg–Churchill train"), both operated by [Via Rail](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail"). *The Canadian* is a transcontinental service linking Toronto with Vancouver.

The main Saskatchewan waterways are the [North Saskatchewan River](/wiki/North_Saskatchewan_River "North Saskatchewan River") or [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River") routes. In total, there are 3,050 bridges maintained by the Department of Highways in Saskatchewan.[[78]](#cite_note-Bridge-80) There are currently twelve ferry services operating in the province, all under the jurisdiction of the Department of Highways.

The [Saskatoon Airport](/wiki/Saskatoon/John_G._Diefenbaker_International_Airport "Saskatoon/John G. Diefenbaker International Airport") was initially established as part of the [Royal Canadian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force "Royal Canadian Air Force") training program during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It was renamed the John G. Diefenbaker Airport in 1993.[[79]](#cite_note-SE-81) *Roland J. Groome Airfield* is the official designation for the [Regina International Airport](/wiki/Regina_International_Airport "Regina International Airport") as of 2005; the airport was established in 1930.

Airlines offering service to Saskatchewan are [Air Canada](/wiki/Air_Canada "Air Canada"), [WestJet](/wiki/WestJet "WestJet"), [Delta Air Lines](/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines"), [Transwest Air](/wiki/Transwest_Air "Transwest Air"), [Sunwing Airlines](/wiki/Sunwing_Airlines "Sunwing Airlines"), Norcanair Airlines, La Ronge Aviation Services Ltd, La Loche Airways, Osprey Wings Ltd, Buffalo Narrows Airways Ltd, Île-à-la-Crosse Airways Ltd, Voyage Air, [Pronto Airways](/wiki/Pronto_Airways "Pronto Airways"), Venture Air Ltd, Pelican Narrows Air Service, Jackson Air Services Ltd, and Northern Dene Airways Ltd.[[80]](#cite_note-service-82)

The [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") agreed to contribute $20 million for two new interchanges in [Saskatoon](/wiki/Saskatoon "Saskatoon"). One of them being at the [Highway 219](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_219 "Saskatchewan Highway 219")/Lorne Avenue intersection with Circle Drive, the other at the Senator Sid Buckwold Bridge (Idylwyld Freeway) and Circle Drive. This is part of the Asia-Pacific Gateway and Corridor Initiative to improve access to the CNR's intermodal freight terminal thereby increasing Asia-Pacific trade. Also, the Government of Canada will contribute $27 million to Regina to construct a CPR intermodal facility and improve infrastructure transportation to the facility from both national highway networks, [Highway 1, the Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_1 "Saskatchewan Highway 1") and [Highway 11, Louis Riel Trail](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_11 "Saskatchewan Highway 11"). This also is part of the Asia-Pacific Gateway and Corridor Initiative to improve access to the CPR terminal and increase Asia-Pacific trade.[[81]](#cite_note-Hon._Lawrence_Cannon,_M.P.,_P.C._Minister_of_transport,_infrastructure_and_communities-83)

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Art")]

Further information: [Culture of Saskatchewan § Visual arts](/wiki/Culture_of_Saskatchewan#Visual_arts "Culture of Saskatchewan")

The province is home to several art galleries, including [MacKenzie Art Gallery](/wiki/MacKenzie_Art_Gallery "MacKenzie Art Gallery"), and [Remai Modern](/wiki/Remai_Modern "Remai Modern"). The province is also home to several [performing arts centres](/wiki/Performing_arts_centre "Performing arts centre") including the [Conexus Arts Centre](/wiki/Conexus_Arts_Centre "Conexus Arts Centre") in Regina, and [TCU Place](/wiki/TCU_Place "TCU Place") in Saskatoon. [PAVED Arts](/wiki/PAVED_Arts "PAVED Arts"), a [new media](/wiki/New_media_art "New media art") artist-run space, is also in Saskatoon.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Music")]

Further information: [Music of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Music_of_Saskatchewan "Music of Saskatchewan")

The province is presently home to several concert [orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), the [Regina Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Regina_Symphony_Orchestra "Regina Symphony Orchestra"), the [Saskatoon Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Saskatoon_Symphony_Orchestra "Saskatoon Symphony Orchestra"), and the [Saskatoon Youth Orchestra](/wiki/Saskatoon_Youth_Orchestra "Saskatoon Youth Orchestra"). The Regina Symphony Orchestra is at the Conexus Arts Centre, while the Saskatoon performs at TCU Place.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Literature")]

Further information: [Culture of Saskatchewan § Literature](/wiki/Culture_of_Saskatchewan#Literature "Culture of Saskatchewan"), [Culture of Saskatchewan § Theatre](/wiki/Culture_of_Saskatchewan#Theatre "Culture of Saskatchewan"), and [List of writers from Saskatchewan](/wiki/List_of_writers_from_Saskatchewan "List of writers from Saskatchewan")

A leading writer from Saskatchewan is [W. O. Mitchell](/wiki/W._O._Mitchell "W. O. Mitchell") (1914–1998), born in [Weyburn](/wiki/Weyburn "Weyburn"). His best-loved novel is *[Who Has Seen the Wind](/wiki/Who_Has_Seen_the_Wind_(novel) "Who Has Seen the Wind (novel)")* (1947), which portrays life on the [Canadian Prairies](/wiki/Canadian_Prairies "Canadian Prairies") and sold almost a million copies in Canada.[[82]](#cite_note-CBCbooks-84) As a broadcaster, he is known for his radio series [Jake and the Kid](/wiki/Jake_and_the_Kid "Jake and the Kid"), which aired on [CBC Radio](/wiki/CBC_Radio "CBC Radio") between 1950 and 1956 and was also about life on the Prairies.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Sports")]

Further information: [Sport in Saskatchewan](/wiki/Sport_in_Saskatchewan "Sport in Saskatchewan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Mosaic_Stadium_Exterior.jpg/250px-Mosaic_Stadium_Exterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosaic_Stadium_Exterior.jpg)

[Mosaic Stadium](/wiki/Mosaic_Stadium "Mosaic Stadium") is the home stadium for the [Saskatchewan Roughriders](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Roughriders "Saskatchewan Roughriders"), a professional [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") team.

[Hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") is the most popular sport in Saskatchewan. More than 500 [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) players have been born in Saskatchewan, the highest per capita output of any Canadian province, U.S. state, or [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") country.[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86) This includes [Gordie Howe](/wiki/Gordie_Howe "Gordie Howe"), dubbed "Mr. Hockey" and widely regarded as one of the greatest hockey players of all time.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88) Some other notable NHL figures born in Saskatchewan include [Keith Allen](/wiki/Keith_Allen_(ice_hockey) "Keith Allen (ice hockey)"), [Bryan Trottier](/wiki/Bryan_Trottier "Bryan Trottier"), [Bernie Federko](/wiki/Bernie_Federko "Bernie Federko"), [Clark Gillies](/wiki/Clark_Gillies "Clark Gillies"), [Fernie Flaman](/wiki/Fernie_Flaman "Fernie Flaman"), [Fred Sasakamoose](/wiki/Fred_Sasakamoose "Fred Sasakamoose"), [Bert Olmstead](/wiki/Bert_Olmstead "Bert Olmstead"), [Harry Watson](/wiki/Harry_Watson_(ice_hockey_b._1923) "Harry Watson (ice hockey b. 1923)"), [Elmer Lach](/wiki/Elmer_Lach "Elmer Lach"), [Max Bentley](/wiki/Max_Bentley "Max Bentley"), [Sid Abel](/wiki/Sid_Abel "Sid Abel"), [Doug Bentley](/wiki/Doug_Bentley "Doug Bentley"), [Eddie Shore](/wiki/Eddie_Shore "Eddie Shore"), [Clint Smith](/wiki/Clint_Smith "Clint Smith"), [Bryan Hextall](/wiki/Bryan_Hextall "Bryan Hextall"), [Johnny Bower](/wiki/Johnny_Bower "Johnny Bower"), [Emile Francis](/wiki/Emile_Francis "Emile Francis"), [Glenn Hall](/wiki/Glenn_Hall "Glenn Hall"), [Chuck Rayner](/wiki/Chuck_Rayner "Chuck Rayner"), [Wendel Clark](/wiki/Wendel_Clark "Wendel Clark"), [Brad McCrimmon](/wiki/Brad_McCrimmon "Brad McCrimmon"), [Mike Babcock](/wiki/Mike_Babcock "Mike Babcock"), [Patrick Marleau](/wiki/Patrick_Marleau "Patrick Marleau"), [Theo Fleury](/wiki/Theoren_Fleury "Theoren Fleury"), [Terry Harper](/wiki/Terry_Harper "Terry Harper"), [Wade Redden](/wiki/Wade_Redden "Wade Redden"), [Brian Propp](/wiki/Brian_Propp "Brian Propp"), [Ryan Getzlaf](/wiki/Ryan_Getzlaf "Ryan Getzlaf"), [Chris Kunitz](/wiki/Chris_Kunitz "Chris Kunitz"), [Kelly Chase](/wiki/Kelly_Chase "Kelly Chase"), and [Jordan Eberle](/wiki/Jordan_Eberle "Jordan Eberle"). A number of prominent women's hockey players and figures have come from the province as well, including [Hayley Wickenheiser](/wiki/Hayley_Wickenheiser "Hayley Wickenheiser"), [Colleen Sostorics](/wiki/Colleen_Sostorics "Colleen Sostorics"), [Gina Kingsbury](/wiki/Gina_Kingsbury "Gina Kingsbury"), [Shannon Miller](/wiki/Shannon_Miller_(ice_hockey) "Shannon Miller (ice hockey)"), and [Emily Clark](/wiki/Emily_Clark_(ice_hockey) "Emily Clark (ice hockey)"). Wickenheiser was the first female skater to play full-time professional hockey in a men's league and is regarded as one of the greatest hockey players of all time.[[87]](#cite_note-89) Saskatchewan does not have a professional hockey franchise, but five teams in the [junior](/wiki/Junior_ice_hockey "Junior ice hockey") [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League") are based in the province: the [Moose Jaw Warriors](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_Warriors "Moose Jaw Warriors"), [Prince Albert Raiders](/wiki/Prince_Albert_Raiders "Prince Albert Raiders"), [Regina Pats](/wiki/Regina_Pats "Regina Pats"), [Saskatoon Blades](/wiki/Saskatoon_Blades "Saskatoon Blades"), and [Swift Current Broncos](/wiki/Swift_Current_Broncos "Swift Current Broncos").

The [Saskatchewan Roughriders](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Roughriders "Saskatchewan Roughriders") are the province's professional [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") team playing in the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League"), and are based in Regina but popular across Saskatchewan. The team's fans are also found to congregate on game days throughout Canada, and collectively they are known as "Rider Nation". The Roughriders are one of the oldest professional sports teams and community-owned franchises in North America and have won five [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup") championships. The province also has successful women's football teams. The [Saskatoon Valkyries](/wiki/Saskatoon_Valkyries "Saskatoon Valkyries") and the [Regina Riot](/wiki/Regina_Riot_(football) "Regina Riot (football)") are the only two teams to win championships in the [Western Women's Canadian Football League](/wiki/Western_Women%27s_Canadian_Football_League "Western Women's Canadian Football League") since it began play in 2011.

The province is home to two other professional sports franchises. The [Saskatchewan Rush](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Rush "Saskatchewan Rush") play in the [National Lacrosse League](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League"). In 2016, their first year after relocating from [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), Alberta, the Rush won both their division title and the league championship. In 2018, the province received a [Canadian Elite Basketball League](/wiki/Canadian_Elite_Basketball_League "Canadian Elite Basketball League") franchise, the [Saskatchewan Rattlers](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Rattlers "Saskatchewan Rattlers"), which won the league's inaugural championship in 2019. The [Saskatchewan Heat](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Heat "Saskatchewan Heat") are a semi-professional team in the [National Ringette League](/wiki/National_Ringette_League "National Ringette League"). The province has five teams in the [Western Canadian Baseball League](/wiki/Western_Canadian_Baseball_League "Western Canadian Baseball League").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Emma_Lake_area_2014-12-31_16-03-32_0039_KC_%2815556369354%29.jpg/250px-Emma_Lake_area_2014-12-31_16-03-32_0039_KC_%2815556369354%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emma_Lake_area_2014-12-31_16-03-32_0039_KC_(15556369354).jpg)

A [pond hockey](/wiki/Pond_hockey "Pond hockey") rink set up on [Emma Lake](/wiki/Emma_Lake_(Saskatchewan) "Emma Lake (Saskatchewan)")

[Curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling") is the province's official sport and, historically, Saskatchewan has been one of the strongest curling provinces.[[88]](#cite_note-90) Teams from Saskatchewan have won seven [Canadian men's championships](/wiki/The_Brier "The Brier"), five [world men's championships](/wiki/List_of_World_Men%27s_Curling_Champions "List of World Men's Curling Champions"), eleven [Canadian women's championships](/wiki/Scotties_Tournament_of_Hearts "Scotties Tournament of Hearts"), and four [world women's championships](/wiki/List_of_World_Women%27s_Curling_Champions "List of World Women's Curling Champions"). Notable curlers from Saskatchewan include [Ernie Richardson](/wiki/Ernie_Richardson_(curler) "Ernie Richardson (curler)"), [Joyce McKee](/wiki/Joyce_McKee "Joyce McKee"), [Vera Pezer](/wiki/Vera_Pezer "Vera Pezer"), [Rick Folk](/wiki/Rick_Folk "Rick Folk"), [Sandra Schmirler](/wiki/Sandra_Schmirler "Sandra Schmirler"), and [Ben Hebert](/wiki/Ben_Hebert "Ben Hebert"). In a 2019 poll conducted by [The Sports Network](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") (TSN), experts ranked Schmirler's Saskatchewan team, which won the gold medal at the [1998 Olympics](/wiki/Curling_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), as the greatest women's team in Canada's history.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

## Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Symbols")]

See also: [Symbols of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Symbols_of_Saskatchewan "Symbols of Saskatchewan")

[![Thread count: black 1 green 6 brown 11 gold 26 red 2 yellow 1 red 2 gold 26 brown 11 green 6 black 1 white 2](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Tartan_of_Saskatchewan.png/250px-Tartan_of_Saskatchewan.png)](/wiki/File:Tartan_of_Saskatchewan.png)

The official tartan of Saskatchewan, created in 1961

The [flag of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Flag_of_Saskatchewan "Flag of Saskatchewan") was officially adopted on September 22, 1969.[[90]](#cite_note-92) The flag features the [provincial shield](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Saskatchewan "Coat of arms of Saskatchewan") in the upper quarter nearest the staff, with the [floral emblem](/wiki/Floral_emblem "Floral emblem"), the [Prairie lily](/wiki/Lilium_philadelphicum "Lilium philadelphicum"), in the fly. The upper green (in [forest green](/wiki/Forest_green "Forest green")) half of the flag represents the northern Saskatchewan forest lands, while the golden lower half of the flag symbolizes the southern wheat fields and prairies. A province-wide competition was held to design the flag, and drew over 4,000 entries. The winning design was by Anthony Drake, then living in [Hodgeville](/wiki/Hodgeville,_Saskatchewan "Hodgeville, Saskatchewan").[[91]](#cite_note-93)

In 2005, Saskatchewan Environment held a province-wide vote to recognize Saskatchewan's centennial year, receiving more than 10,000 online and mail-in votes from the public. The [walleye](/wiki/Walleye "Walleye") was the overwhelming favourite of the six native fish species nominated for the designation, receiving more than half the votes cast.[[92]](#cite_note-94) Other species in the running were the [lake sturgeon](/wiki/Lake_sturgeon "Lake sturgeon"), [lake trout](/wiki/Lake_trout "Lake trout"), [lake whitefish](/wiki/Lake_whitefish "Lake whitefish"), [northern pike](/wiki/Northern_pike "Northern pike") and [yellow perch](/wiki/Yellow_perch "Yellow perch").

Saskatchewan's other symbols include the tartan, the licence plate, and the provincial flower. Saskatchewan's official [tartan](/wiki/Tartan "Tartan") was registered with the Court of [Lord Lyon King of Arms](/wiki/Lord_Lyon_King_of_Arms "Lord Lyon King of Arms") in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in 1961. It has seven colours: gold, brown, green, red, yellow, white and black. The provincial licence plates display the slogan "Land of Living Skies". The provincial flower of Saskatchewan is the [western red lily](/wiki/Western_red_lily "Western red lily").

### Centennial celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Centennial celebrations")]

In 2005, Saskatchewan celebrated its centennial. To honour it, the [Royal Canadian Mint](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mint "Royal Canadian Mint") issued a commemorative five-dollar coin depicting Canada's wheat fields as well as a circulation [25-cent coin](/wiki/Quarter_(Canadian_coin) "Quarter (Canadian coin)") of a similar design. Queen Elizabeth II and [Prince Philip](/wiki/Prince_Philip "Prince Philip") visited [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan"), [Saskatoon](/wiki/Saskatoon "Saskatoon"), and [Lumsden](/wiki/Lumsden,_Saskatchewan "Lumsden, Saskatchewan"), and the Saskatchewan-reared [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell") issued an album in Saskatchewan's honour.
